title: TPU v1 · Two Register Classes, Two Reset Strategies: Reset Control, Initialize the Datapath by Protocol
oneLine: Within one TPU, control registers such as FSM state and counters must reset to known values, while 65k+ MAC elements and the 4 MiB accumulator storage can rely on an explicit clear-before-accumulate sequence. The distinction shows which state requires hardware reset and which reset wiring can be safely avoided.

legend.name: Control registers (reset)
legend.desc: FSM state, counters, and configuration state must enter known values such as IDLE or zero after reset. Otherwise simulation propagates X and silicon bring-up may fail. Their count is small enough that reset is justified.

legend.name: Accumulator state (no reset)
legend.desc: The systolic array contains 65k+ MAC elements and 4 MiB of 32-bit accumulator storage. When the computation protocol explicitly clears this state before every use, direct reset wiring is often unnecessary and a very large reset network can be avoided.

legend.name: Physical reset network
legend.desc: Reset must be distributed with controlled skew and fanout to every resettable flop, much like a high-fanout tree. Limiting reset to essential control state reduces routing, load, area, and timing impact.

legend.name: Reset release (synchronized)
legend.desc: A common scheme is asynchronous assertion with synchronous deassertion: reset takes effect immediately, but release passes through a two-flop synchronizer in each clock domain to reduce metastability risk.

steps.cap: ① Identify state that must be known: control FSMs, counters, and configuration registers must settle at IDLE or zero so bring-up can begin deterministically.
② Identify datapath state that need not be directly reset: the MAC and accumulator array is explicitly cleared before accumulation, so reset wiring would add cost without changing the protocol.
③ The benefit is physical: the reset network reaches only essential control state, not 65k+ MAC elements—saving area and routing and reducing high-fanout timing burden.
④ Define release for the state that is reset: asynchronously assert and synchronously deassert in each clock domain to avoid unsafe release near a clock edge.
⑤ Apply a documented reset policy across the design. Polarity, assertion style, release synchronization, and exceptions must be deliberate and consistently verified.

before: Define a project reset policy: specify synchronous or asynchronous assertion where appropriate, active-high or active-low polarity, and synchronized release. Uncontrolled variation complicates CDC/RDC, synthesis, and formal verification.

Partition reset state: identify registers that must enter a known value (FSMs, counters, configuration) and datapath state that is initialized by protocol (large accumulator arrays). This choice determines reset-network scale.
Fix clock and width conventions: define the active clock edge, 8-bit PE multiplication, 32-bit accumulation, and instruction/configuration widths in the coding standard.
Define reset deassertion: provide a reviewed reset synchronizer for asynchronous assertion and synchronous release, instantiated independently in each clock domain.
Constrain synthesizable reset logic: reset arrives through approved reset ports and structures, not arbitrary data logic. Sequential state uses always_ff; unintended latches are prohibited.
during: Use reset for control state. Example: always_ff @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) if(!rst_n) st<=IDLE; else st<=nxt;. FSM state and counters need a reset branch that establishes deterministic behavior. This is standard illustrative RTL, not proprietary TPU source.

Omit direct reset from protocol-initialized datapath state. Example: always_ff @(posedge clk) acc<=acc+a*b;. The clear-before-compute operation establishes validity, allowing tens of thousands of accumulator registers to avoid reset wiring.
Keep reset conventions controlled: do not mix polarity and release behavior without an explicit architecture reason. Every exception must be documented and covered by RDC and verification.
Synchronize asynchronous reset release: pass deassertion through two flops clocked by the destination domain. Releasing near a clock edge without synchronization can metastably split FSM state.
Understand the cost of missing essential reset: a control register without initialization remains X in RTL simulation and powers up unpredictably in silicon, which can block boot and bring-up.

Do not reset the entire datapath merely for reassurance: resetting state that the protocol already overwrites creates a large high-fanout network, increases area and routing, and may reduce frequency. Omitting it is an engineered trade-off, not a shortcut.

result.label: Reset control register (example)
result.value: always_ff @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) if(!rst_n) st<=IDLE;

result.label: Accumulator without direct reset (example)
result.value: always_ff @(posedge clk) acc<=acc+a*b;  // clear by protocol first

result.label: Reset policy
result.value: One documented policy for polarity, assertion, and release

result.label: Reset-network scale
result.value: Control state only; no direct reset to 65k+ MAC / 4 MiB accumulator state

result.label: Datapath convention
result.value: 8-bit multiply, 32-bit accumulate; sequential state in always_ff

after: Run lint and reset rules: check policy consistency, missing reset on essential control state, unintended latches, and reset signals driven by data logic.

Simulate X and initialization behavior: apply reset and confirm that all control FSMs and counters become known. Release reset and prove that the clear sequence initializes accumulator state before any value is consumed.
Run CDC/RDC analysis: verify a complete reset synchronizer in every clock domain and ensure that no unsynchronized deassertion crosses domain boundaries.
Run trial synthesis: confirm that reset fanout does not dominate timing and that the expected area/routing benefit from unreset datapath state is realized while meeting 700 MHz.
Hand reset requirements to verification: cover known control state after reset, safe deassertion, clear-before-use, and X detection in the Stage ⑥ plan.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (reset and RTL coding examples reflect standard practice, not proprietary TPU source)
